title: Take Up Your Cross
subtitle: Christ calls the crowd, and you among them, to follow Him by the way of the cross

stillness: Be still, for the Lord does not want an unwilling servant.
He compels no one; He counsels, and leaves each one to his own choosing.
Quiet your heart, and come willingly.
gospel: After the Lord had foretold the mystery of His Passion in secret to His disciples,
He called the crowd together with them.
And He said: If anyone wishes to come after Me,
Daily, He says in Luke: let him take up his cross daily.
behold: Consider that the Lord here speaks to all in general, and exhorts all,
for the imitation of His Passion belongs to all, whether by martyrdom or by penance.
He says: If anyone — which marks a difference — wishes to come after Me:
by inward affection, by outward imitation, toward the Father on high.
And He says well, wishes, for He forces no one,
that the service may be more pleasing to the Lord, and the reward more pleasing to the servant.
Behold the right order: that he who would follow the Lord first deny himself, and so take up the cross,
so that first his will be ready to bear every misery.
consider: Let him deny himself, by the renunciation of his own will:
not only his things or his kin, but his own understanding, his own affection, his own sense.
For to renounce one's own will is far greater than to renounce things of this kind.
Prosper asks: what does it profit them to cast away their goods, who do not relinquish their own wills?
Let him take up his cross — by the mortification of his body, by compassion for his neighbor,
and, if it must be, by the endurance of martyrdom.
The first man lost three things through sin: liberty, happiness, and dignity.
These three the Lord here teaches us to recover by three:
liberty, when He says, Let him deny himself;
happiness, when He adds, And take up his cross;
dignity, when He adds, And follow Me.
Wretched is the penance that is delicate — wishing to feel no affliction at all — against which He says: take up the cross.
There is a martyrdom of the body, and there is a martyrdom that is spiritual:
to govern the motions of the soul, to refrain the assaults of the vices,
and daily to do battle against them, joining a kind of quarrel with the inner man.
With Paul let it be said: The world is crucified to me, and I to the world.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, grant me so to deny myself,
that in prosperity and in all things that delight for a time, I may turn away from evil, and seek nothing but Your honor;
and, renouncing my own will, that I may obey You, and for Your sake every creature.
Grant me also to take up my cross,
taking upon myself willingly afflictions and hardships,
and patiently bearing those laid upon me by others,
and having compassion on my neighbors in their needs,
and not ceasing to do good in every adversity.
Grant me also to follow You, imitating Your footsteps;
and, conforming myself to Your life, that I may come after You,
reaching at last the heavenly homeland. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Abneget semetipsum per propriæ voluntatis renuntiationem; non tantum sua vel suos, sed suum intellectum, suum affectum, suum sensum.
latinJewel.english: Let him deny himself by the renunciation of his own will: not only his things or his kin, but his own understanding, his own affection, his own sense.
